deconstruction_targets: The idea that humans or angels could have free-will without the capacity to sin
The notion that grace could be a natural quality inherent in creatures without divine origin
The claim that God would permit sin as part of divine providence for greater goods
constructive_proposals: That humans must use their own counsel and free-will responsibly under God's permission
That grace is necessary to confirm beings in goodness and prevent sin through divine influence
That the universe requires the possibility of sin to allow for moral growth and the demonstration of virtue

scapegoat.name: Satan (the Devil)
attributes: Capable of sinning by nature due to free-will
Permitted by God to sin as part of divine providence
Originator of witchcraft through his fall
scapegoat.justification: Satan is framed as the necessary agent of evil whose capacity for sin and rebellion enables the conditions under which human moral development and divine grace are meaningful; without his sin, the structure of grace, temptation, and redemption would be impossible.

moral_justification: The text argues that God justly permits sin in angels and humans because it allows for genuine free-will, moral growth, and the demonstration of virtue through good works; without the possibility of sin, there can be no true goodness, grace, or redemption.
summary: This theological passage engages in a complex deconstruction of the compatibility between free-will, sin, and divine grace, arguing that creatures cannot naturally possess grace or be incapable of sin without contradicting their nature. It defends God's permission for Satan and humans to sin as necessary for moral development and the operation of grace, positioning Satan as the essential agent of evil whose fall enables human spiritual testing and redemption.
